---
title: "1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
notes: |
  Most backtesting mistakes do not create harmless noise. They create false
  confidence by making the simulated market easier than the real one. In this
  lesson, we will map the most common pitfalls and ask a simple question each
  time: did the backtest use information, securities, prices, or venues that a
  live strategy would not actually have had?

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Most pitfalls make the simulated market easier than the live market.

| Backtest shortcut  | Live-trading reality     | Main risk      |
| ------------------ | ------------------------ | -------------- |
| Future information | Only current information | look-ahead     |
| Surviving stocks   | Delistings and failures  | survivorship   |
| Convenient prices  | Executable prices        | price mismatch |
| Generic quotes     | Venue-specific quotes    | venue mismatch |

::: {.notes}
Open this section with the main warning: most pitfalls bias the result in the
same direction. They make the simulated market easier than the real market.
That can happen through early information, survivor-only securities,
non-executable prices, or the wrong trading venue. So the danger is not random
error. The danger is inflated performance.
:::

## Look-ahead bias enters when today's signal uses tomorrow's information.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/look-ahead-timing.svg){fig-alt="Flow showing that same-day high and low are unknown at the morning decision, so the entry signal cannot use them"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Start with the most basic pitfall: look-ahead bias. It occurs when the backtest
uses future information to make a current decision. A same-day high or low is a
clean example because that value is unknown until the day is over. If the
signal uses it earlier, the backtest is predicting with information the live
system could never have.
:::

## A one-period shift keeps the signal available before the trade.

```python
# unsafe in a same-day signal
signal_t = high_t > threshold

# safer shape
signal_t = raw_signal.shift(1)
```

::: {.notes}
The practical fix is usually simple: delay the signal so the trade uses only
information already known. The point of the shift is not cosmetic. It enforces
the timeline of a real decision, where the signal must exist before the return
is earned.
:::

## Shared backtest and live logic blocks one major source of look-ahead bias.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/shared-strategy-function.svg){fig-alt="Historical feed and live feed both entering one strategy function that produces orders"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Chan's stronger defense is structural, not just procedural. If the backtest and
live system share the same strategy function and only the data feed changes,
future information has fewer places to sneak in. That does not guarantee
profits, but it does remove one major class of backtest-only programming error.
:::

## Different code paths create a backtest-only failure mode.

| If the code path differs                | If only the data feed differs        |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| future columns can enter backtest logic | live constraints discipline the rule |
| simulated rule may not be tradable      | backtest rule is closer to execution |

::: {.notes}
This comparison shows why architecture matters. Separate code paths invite
separate assumptions, and separate assumptions invite bias. When only the feed
changes, live constraints discipline the research code instead of letting the
backtest drift into a fantasy version of the strategy.
:::

## Validation stops being validation after the model is tuned to pass it.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/holdout-tuning-loop.svg){fig-alt="Loop showing holdout testing, poor result, parameter tweaking, and retesting on the same holdout"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The next pitfall is broader: data-snooping bias. A model with enough freedom
can fit noise and then look brilliant in hindsight. Even a holdout sample stops
protecting us if we keep retuning the model until that holdout looks good.
Once that happens, validation has quietly turned into training.
:::

## Simpler linear rules leave fewer places to memorize noise.

| Design choice        | Fitting freedom                | Backtest risk |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------- |
| Many nonlinear rules | many thresholds and exceptions | high          |
| Few linear terms     | fewer knobs to tune            | lower         |
| Equal-weight ranks   | sampling accidents matter less | lower         |

::: {.notes}
This is where Chan's preference for simplicity becomes useful. The value of a
linear rule is not that markets are always linear. The value is that simple
rules leave fewer knobs to tune and fewer ways to memorize noise. A more
complicated model should earn its complexity by improving prediction, not just
improving the historical fit.
:::

## Splits and dividends are accounting events, not trading signals.

| Event                   | Price | Shares | Market value |
| ----------------------- | ----: | -----: | -----------: |
| Before 2-for-1 split    |   100 |      1 |          100 |
| After 2-for-1 split     |    50 |      2 |          100 |
| Unadjusted price series |  -50% |        | false signal |

::: {.notes}
Now move from modeling errors to data preparation errors. Stock splits and
dividends change the quoted price, but they do not represent the same kind of
economic move as a genuine selloff. If the price history is not adjusted before
the signal uses it, the strategy can mistake accounting events for tradable
information.
:::

## A survivor-only database makes recovery look predictable.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/survivorship-database.svg){fig-alt="Database filter showing survived stocks included and delisted or failed stocks missing"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Another common distortion is survivorship bias. If the database contains only
stocks that still exist today, the backtest acts as if the trader somehow knew
which distressed names would survive. That is especially dangerous in
mean-reversion strategies that deliberately buy recent losers.
:::

## Mean-reversion backtests are most exposed to survivor-only universes.

| Mean-reversion trade    | Bias direction       |
| ----------------------- | -------------------- |
| Long the biggest losers | performance inflated |
| Short failed firms      | profits omitted      |

::: {.notes}
This slide makes the direction of the bias concrete. Long loser portfolios can
look too strong because failed firms disappear from history, while some of the
best short opportunities can vanish from the sample entirely. Either way, the
historical universe is cleaner than the one a live trader actually faced.
:::

## Live auction orders require primary-exchange auction prices.

| Order in live trading | Price needed in the backtest    |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Market-on-open        | primary opening auction         |
| Market-on-close       | primary closing auction         |
| Signal from high/low  | representative executable range |

::: {.notes}
Next, connect prices to execution venue. In U.S. equities, market-on-open and
market-on-close orders target the primary exchange auction, not just any print
in the consolidated tape. So the historical price field has to match the order
the strategy would really send.
:::

## Auction strategies fail when consolidated outliers stand in for fills.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/auction-price-mismatch.svg){fig-alt="Comparison showing secondary venue odd print is not equal to primary auction fill"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
If we ignore that distinction, a small and possibly unrepresentative print on a
secondary venue can become a fake entry or exit in the backtest. The strategy
then profits from a price it could not realistically fill. That is why auction
strategies need primary-exchange auction prices, not convenient substitutes.
:::

## Forex backtests are venue-specific because quotes and spreads are venue-specific.

| Venue           | Bid-ask quote   | Tradable by our strategy? |
| --------------- | --------------- | ------------------------- |
| Bank or ECN A   | one spread      | maybe                     |
| Bank or ECN B   | another spread  | maybe                     |
| Aggregated book | combined quotes | only if executable        |

::: {.notes}
The same principle extends even more strongly to foreign exchange. FX is highly
fragmented, spreads differ across venues, and there is no universal best bid or
offer that every trader can access. So a forex backtest is realistic only when
its quotes come from the same executable venue set as the live strategy.
:::

## A clean backtest matches live trading on information, universe, price, and venue.

| Gate        | Passing evidence                                 |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Information | no future fields in current signals              |
| Universe    | delisted securities are represented              |
| Price       | corporate actions and auction prices are handled |
| Venue       | historical quotes match executable venues        |

::: {.notes}
End the section with four gates that summarize the entire pitfall map. Check
the information set, the security universe, the price series, and the trading
venue. If any gate fails, the backtest may still look precise, but it is
describing a market that the live strategy never had access to.
:::
